docs.title: 🍞 Bread
docs.text: A knowledge base and recipe collection for home bread baking, with a focus on sourdough and ancient grains. Built with AI assistance, real recipes, and human feedback — with an emphasis on evidence-based information wherever possible.

docs.title: What's in here
docs.text: File What it covers sourdough.md Sourdough principles: starter readiness, autolyse, bulk fermentation, shaping, baking flours/index.md Flour guides (spelt, rye) — see the flours index recipes/index.md All recipes — see the recipe index

docs.title: How recipes are expressed
docs.text:
All recipes use baker's percentages — every ingredient is expressed as a percentage of total flour weight (flour is always 100%).

Ingredient %  =  (ingredient weight / total flour weight) × 100

Example (450g flour loaf):

Ingredient % Weight Wholemeal spelt flour 100% 450g Water 73% 329g Starter 15% 68g Salt 2% 9g

Baker's percentages make recipes easy to scale: pick any flour weight and multiply everything else by the same factor.

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
If you're new here, start with the wholemeal spelt sourdough recipe. It has everything you need: a full formula, equipment list, step-by-step method, and a baking schedule.

Before you bake, it's worth reading: - Sourdough principles — especially the starter readiness and bulk fermentation sections - Spelt properties — spelt behaves very differently from modern wheat, and knowing why prevents common failures

docs.title: Guiding principles
docs.text:
• Temperature governs timing. Fermentation times are guidelines; dough behaviour is the real signal.
  • Evidence over folklore. Claims here are grounded in what's known about fermentation, gluten chemistry, and food science — not just tradition.
  • Less handling for ancient grains. Spelt, einkorn, and emmer have fragile gluten. Treat them gently.
  • Hydration is flour-dependent. Always test a new bag; absorption varies significantly even within the same variety.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: This project grows through real bakes, human feedback, and iterative improvement. Contributions welcome — whether that's a new recipe, a correction, better evidence for an existing claim, or notes from your own experiments.

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text:
• [ ] More ancient grain guides (einkorn, emmer, rye)
  • [ ] Additional recipes (mixed-grain loaves, enriched breads)
  • [ ] Troubleshooting guide with photos
  • [ ] Evidence references and source links for key claims

docs.title: The Starter
docs.text: A sourdough starter is a live culture of wild yeast and lactic acid bacteria (LAB) maintained in a flour-water mixture. The two organisms work together: - Wild yeast produces CO₂ (rise) and some flavour esters - LAB (primarily Lactobacillus species) produce lactic and acetic acids (flavour and preservation)

docs.title: Starter Readiness
docs.text:
The ideal time to use a starter is at peak activity — when it has doubled (or slightly more) in volume, has a domed top, a bubbly interior, and smells pleasantly tangy/yeasty.

Signs of peak: - Volume has doubled from fed weight - Top is domed, not yet collapsed - Float test: a small spoonful in water floats (adequate gas production) - Smells active and tangy, not overly acidic or alcoholic

A starter past peak (collapsed, liquidy, sharp/alcoholic smell) will still leaven bread but will give a more sour result and weaker rise. Use a peaked starter for the best balance of rise and flavour.

docs.title: Inoculation (Starter Percentage)
docs.text:
The amount of starter added to a dough, expressed as a percentage of total flour weight.

Inoculation Effect Use when 5–10% Slow fermentation, mild flavour, complex tang Long cold bulk or warm kitchen in summer 15–20% Balanced — reliable rise, moderate tang Standard home bake, established starter 20–30% Faster fermentation, more sour Young/less active starter, cold kitchen

Default recommendation: 15% — a good balance for an established, peaked starter at typical UK room temperatures (18–22°C).

For spelt specifically, 15% is appropriate. Spelt ferments faster than wheat, so you do not need to compensate with a higher inoculation.

docs.title: Rye Starters
docs.text:
Rye flour produces exceptionally active starters. The bran carries a dense, diverse population of wild yeasts and lactic acid bacteria, and rye's naturally high enzyme activity converts starches to sugars rapidly — giving the microorganisms more food, faster. This is why a neglected wheat starter often responds well to a single rye feed.

Hydration: A rye starter is typically maintained at 100% hydration, but this does not mean it will look or behave like a liquid wheat starter. Because rye's pentosans (water-binding polysaccharides) absorb several times their weight in water, a 100% rye starter forms a thick, pasty consistency — similar to wet beach sand or hummus — rather than a pourable liquid. If a rye starter looks thin, it has probably over-fermented or needs more flour.

Behaviour differences from wheat: - Peaks faster — especially in a warm kitchen, it can double within 3–4 hours - Smells more assertively sour and earthy, less yeasty-sweet than a wheat starter - Has a shorter window at peak before collapsing — use it on the way up or at peak, not after

Using rye to boost a wheat starter: Adding 10–20% rye flour to a wheat starter feed (by weight, replacing an equivalent amount of the wheat flour) increases activity noticeably without fundamentally changing the starter's character. This is useful in cold kitchens or with a sluggish starter.

The German tradition (Anstellgut): Traditional German rye breadmaking maintains a fully mature rye starter — Anstellgut — at around 80–100% hydration, used at peak when it reaches a pH of approximately 3.9–4.2. This level of acidity is not merely for flavour; it is essential for controlling rye's amylase enzymes during baking (see rye.md for detail).

docs.title: Autolyse
docs.text:
Autolyse is a rest period for flour + water only, before the starter or salt are added.

Purpose: 1. Full hydration of the flour (especially important for wholemeal — bran absorbs slowly) 2. Gluten begins to develop passively without mechanical energy 3. Enzymes begin breaking down starches, improving flavour 4. Results in a more extensible, easier-to-shape dough with less mixing required

Method: 1. Mix flour and water (hold back ~5% of water to use for dissolving salt later) 2. Mix until no dry flour remains — do not over-mix 3. Cover and rest at room temperature

Duration: - White flour: 20–60 minutes - Wholemeal/whole grain: 30–60 minutes (bran needs longer to hydrate) - Wholemeal spelt: 30–45 minutes (enough to hydrate bran; longer risks over-relaxing the already-extensible gluten)

Do not add starter or salt during autolyse — starter accelerates fermentation (shortcutting the enzyme activity) and salt tightens gluten and slows hydration.

docs.title: Adding the Starter
docs.text: After autolyse: 1. Add the starter on top of the autolysed dough 2. Use the pincer method (squeeze through fingers) or fold-and-press until fully incorporated — ~2–3 minutes 3. Rest 20–30 minutes (allows starter to begin distributing its activity before salt goes in)

docs.title: Adding Salt
docs.text:
Salt is added after the starter is incorporated and has had a short rest.

  1. Dissolve salt in the reserved water (this helps distribution)
  2. Pour over dough, incorporate with pincer method or folds
  3. Salt tightens gluten — the dough will feel noticeably firmer after incorporation

Salt percentage: 1.8–2% is the standard range. 2% is common in home baking; professional bakers sometimes use 1.8% to allow slightly faster fermentation. Do not reduce below 1.8% — salt is essential for flavour, crust colour (Maillard reaction), and controlling fermentation rate.

docs.title: Bulk Fermentation
docs.text: The first rise, from when the starter is incorporated until the dough is shaped. This is where most flavour development and gas production occurs.

docs.title: What to watch for (not the clock)
docs.text:
The bulk is complete when: - Dough has increased 40–50% in volume (for spelt; wheat targets 75–100%) - Edges where dough meets the container look slightly domed (convex) - Dough jiggles as a cohesive mass when the container is shaken - Surface has bubbles and looks alive - Dough feels lighter and more airy when handled

At 20–22°C with 15% inoculation: expect 3.5–5 hours for wholemeal spelt. At 24°C+, this can drop to 2.5–3.5 hours. Use visual and tactile cues, not the clock alone.

docs.title: Stretch and Folds
docs.text:
Performed during the first half of bulk fermentation to build strength in the dough.

Standard stretch and fold: - Wet your hand, grab one side of the dough, stretch it up until resistance is felt, fold over to the opposite side - Rotate bowl 90° and repeat — four folds = one set - Cover and rest 30 minutes between sets

For wholemeal spelt: 3 sets, then leave the dough undisturbed. The fragile gluten does not benefit from extra sets.

Coil fold (gentler, preferred for fragile doughs): - Slide hands under the dough, lift it from the centre - Let each end fold down as you lower it back - Rotate 90°, repeat once more — that is one coil fold set

Lamination (advanced — used for open-crumb white sourdoughs):

Lamination builds structure through thin, layered stretching rather than repeated folding. It is performed once, roughly halfway through bulk fermentation, in place of further stretch and folds.

  1. Wet a large work surface generously
  2. Pour the dough out and, using wet hands, gently stretch outward in all directions to form a large, thin rectangle — aim for almost translucent in places
  3. Fold one third over the centre, then the opposite third on top (letter fold — like folding a letter into thirds)
  4. Rotate 90°, letter-fold again
  5. Return the dough to its container

If the dough tears before it is fully stretched, stop and rest it for 5 minutes before continuing. After lamination, the dough is delicate — continue with coil folds only, not stretch-and-folds, which are too aggressive at this stage.

After the final set, leave the dough completely undisturbed until bulk is complete.

docs.title: Pre-Shape and Bench Rest
docs.text:
1. Turn the dough onto an unfloured surface
  2. Using a bench scraper and your hand, drag the dough towards you to build surface tension — do this 2–3 times, gently
  3. Cover (inverted bowl or damp cloth) and bench rest for 20–30 minutes

The bench rest relaxes the gluten enough for final shaping. For spelt, keep this brief — you don't want the dough to relax so much it loses the tension you just built.

docs.title: Final Shape
docs.text:
For a round (boule): 1. Flip the dough, stretch gently into a rough rectangle 2. Fold the top third down, bottom third up (letter fold), then roll towards you while dragging to build tension 3. Place seam-side up in a well-floured banneton (rice flour is best — it doesn't absorb into the dough)

For spelt: shape firmly but quickly. Every additional handling step degrades the gluten. One confident shape is better than repeatedly adjusting.

docs.title: Cold Retard (Overnight Proof)
docs.text:
Place the shaped loaf (in its banneton, covered) in the fridge at 4–5°C.

Duration: 8–16 hours is the typical range. The cold retard: - Slows fermentation dramatically, giving a long, slow final proof - Develops flavour (acetic acid production increases at cold temperatures) - Firms the dough for easier scoring - Allows you to bake at a convenient time

For spelt, a cold retard is strongly recommended — the cold firms the dough and compensates for the weaker gluten structure, giving better oven spring.

Bake directly from the fridge — do not warm the dough before baking.

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
A lidded cast iron vessel (Dutch oven) is the most reliable home method. It traps steam from the dough in the first phase of baking, which: - Keeps the crust extensible so the loaf can expand fully - Develops a thin, crackly crust rather than a thick, tough one - Promotes better oven spring

Preheat the Dutch oven in the oven at 250°C (230°C fan) for at least 45 minutes before baking.

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Score the cold loaf just before baking (a single ear score at ~30–45° angle, or a simple cross)
  2. Lower into the hot Dutch oven, replace lid immediately
  3. Bake with lid on: 20 minutes — steam phase, maximum oven spring
  4. Remove lid, reduce to 220°C (200°C fan): 20–25 minutes — crust development and browning
  5. Loaf is done when it sounds hollow when tapped on the base, and an instant-read thermometer reads 96–98°C internal temperature

docs.title: Cooling
docs.text: Cool on a wire rack for at least 1 hour before cutting. The crumb is still setting during this time — cutting too early gives a gummy interior.

docs.title: Diagnosing Common Problems
docs.text: Problem Likely cause Flat loaf, no oven spring Over-fermented bulk; starter past peak; under-scored Dense, gummy crumb Under-fermented; cut while still hot Very sour flavour Over-fermented; starter past peak when used; too high inoculation Collapsed dough, impossible to shape Spelt over-fermented; too long bulk or too warm Thick, tough crust Insufficient steam; baked without lid Pale crust Oven not hot enough; insufficient Maillard reaction

docs.title: Flours
docs.text:
Reference guides for the flours used in this collection.

File What it covers spelt.md Spelt flour properties, gluten behaviour, hydration guidance, handling rules rye.md Rye flour properties, pentosans, enzyme activity, types, hydration, handling rules

docs.title: What Rye Is
docs.text:
Rye (Secale cereale) is a cereal grain that arrived in Northern and Eastern Europe largely by accident. It grew as a weed in wheat and barley fields — a botanical gate-crasher — before bakers realised it made remarkable bread in its own right. Its domestication is dated to around 9,500–8,500 years ago in eastern Turkey and western Armenia, but it only became a primary crop as it spread north and west into climates where wheat struggled.

The reason for rye's dominance across Germany, Scandinavia, Poland, and Russia is simple: it survives. Rye tolerates thin, acidic, sandy soils and hard winters that would kill a wheat crop. For centuries it was the grain of the north — pumpernickel, rugbrød, Borodinsky — while wheat remained a warmer-climate luxury. Today it is also grown in the UK, particularly in East Anglia, though it remains a niche grain compared to bread wheat.

Rye's flavour is unmistakable: deep, earthy, slightly sweet, with a tartness that pairs naturally with sourdough fermentation. It is arguably the most flavourful grain a home baker can work with. It is also the most technically demanding.

docs.title: Pentosans: Not Gluten
docs.text:
The single most important thing to understand about rye is that it does not form a gluten network. Rye contains gliadin and glutenin proteins, but in insufficient quantities to build the kind of elastic, extensible network that makes wheat dough workable. What rye has instead, in abundance, are pentosans — specifically arabinoxylans, water-binding polysaccharides that make up 8–12% of the rye grain's weight.

Pentosans are hydrophilic to an almost absurd degree. They can absorb up to ten times their own weight in water, and rye flour can absorb as much as eight times more total water than wheat flour at equivalent weights. When hydrated, pentosans form a viscous, gel-like network throughout the dough. This gel does the structural work that gluten would do in wheat — holding gas bubbles during fermentation, supporting the crumb as it bakes — but it behaves completely differently.

Property Rye (pentosan gel) Wheat (gluten network) Structure Viscous gel Elastic web Extensibility Flows under pressure Stretches and returns Stickiness Very high Low–moderate Water requirement Extremely high Moderate Response to handling Doesn't develop with work Strengthens with kneading

The practical consequence: rye dough cannot be kneaded into shape. The more you handle it, the stickier and more unworkable it becomes. High-percentage rye doughs are mixed until combined, poured or scooped into a tin, and baked — not shaped by hand. The technique is entirely different from wheat.

docs.title: Enzyme Activity: Why Sourdough Is Not Optional
docs.text:
Rye has naturally high alpha-amylase activity — significantly higher than wheat. This matters enormously in baking.

Alpha-amylase breaks starch down into fermentable sugars. During baking, as the dough heats up, two things happen simultaneously: starch begins to gelatinise (setting the crumb), and amylase activity peaks. In wheat, the amylases are deactivated early enough that the starch sets before it is significantly degraded. In rye, amylase activity continues longer and at higher levels, and can break down the gelatinising starch faster than it sets — resulting in what German bakers call Backschaden (bake damage): a gummy, collapsed, structurally ruined crumb.

The fix is acid. The pH optimum for alpha-amylase is 5.0–5.5. Sourdough fermentation drives the dough to a pH of approximately 4.0–4.5, well below the enzyme's active range. At this acidity, amylase activity is suppressed enough that the starch can gelatinise and set properly.

This is not a flavour consideration — it is a structural necessity. A high-percentage rye bread baked without adequate acidification will be gummy and unsliceable regardless of baking technique. For any loaf with more than about 50% rye, proper sourdough fermentation (or, in traditional practice, an added acid like vinegar) is not optional.

docs.title: The Falling Number
docs.text:
The falling number test (Hagberg–Perten number) is the standard measure of amylase activity in flour. It measures how quickly enzyme activity liquefies a starch gel:

Falling Number Enzyme activity Baking implication Above 200 seconds Low Good 150–200 seconds Optimal Ideal for breadmaking 80–150 seconds High Bread risk; rely heavily on sourdough acid Below 80 seconds Very high Likely sprouted grain; avoid or blend

Rye is particularly prone to elevated falling numbers in wet harvest years, when grain begins to sprout in the field. When buying rye flour, milling date and falling number (if disclosed) are worth knowing.

docs.title: Types of Rye Flour
docs.text:
Rye flour is classified by extraction rate — how much of the whole grain is retained in the flour. Higher extraction means more bran, more pentosans, stronger flavour, and higher water demand.

Type Extraction Ash content Character Light/white rye 65–70% ~0.6% Mild, neutral; mostly starchy endosperm Medium rye ~85% ~1.4% Balanced; robust, complex flavour Dark rye ~95% ~2.0% Intense, earthy; high pentosan content Wholemeal/wholegrain rye 100% Full grain Maximum flavour and nutrition; most demanding

UK packaging is inconsistent with these grades. "Wholemeal rye" is reliable; "dark rye" and "light rye" are less standardised and worth reading the label for extraction rate or ash content if given.

Medium rye is a good starting point for most home bakers — it delivers genuine rye flavour without the full handling challenge of 100% wholemeal. Wholemeal rye is the most rewarding once you are comfortable with how rye behaves.

docs.title: Stoneground Rye
docs.text:
Stone milling grinds the entire grain — bran, germ, and endosperm — together at slow speed, generating less heat than roller milling. The result is flour with:

  • Germ oils intact, distributed through the flour rather than separated out. These oils carry nutty, earthy flavour compounds that are distinctive to fresh stoneground flour.
  • Irregular particle size — bran particles are smaller and harder to sift out, which is why stoneground wholemeal looks more thoroughly integrated than roller-milled wholemeal.
  • Faster staling in the bag — because the oils are present, stoneground rye flour goes rancid faster than roller-milled. Buy in quantities you will use within 6–8 weeks, store cool and dark.

The flavour difference between stoneground and roller-milled rye is pronounced. Stoneground is more assertive — some find it almost overwhelming, in the best possible way. If you want the full character of rye in a loaf, stoneground is the choice.

docs.title: Organic Rye
docs.text:
Rye grain, like all cereals, carries a natural population of wild yeasts and lactic acid bacteria on the bran surface. In organic production, no fungicides or pesticides are applied, so these populations survive the harvest and milling process — and end up in your flour.

This matters for sourdough starters in particular. Organic rye flour contains more diverse and active microbial populations than conventionally grown flour, which is one reason a rye-fed starter built from organic flour tends to be exceptionally vigorous. It is also why a new starter made with organic rye flour typically becomes active faster than one made with conventional flour.

For bread flavour, organic wholemeal rye delivers the most complex results — the terroir of the grain, the character of the soil and season, comes through in a way that heavily processed flour cannot match.

docs.title: Hydration Guidance
docs.text:
Because pentosans absorb water so aggressively, rye requires significantly more water than wheat. As a rough rule: for every 10% of wheat flour you replace with rye, increase total hydration by 5–8 percentage points.

Rye content Typical hydration range Dough feel 10–20% 65–72% Close to wheat dough; slight increase in stickiness 30–50% 72–82% Noticeably stickier; less shapeable by hand 60–80% 82–90% Very sticky; tin-baked, not free-formed 90–100% 90–100%+ Paste-like; mixed, poured into tin, no shaping

These ranges shift depending on extraction rate. Wholemeal rye at 100% needs more water than light rye at 100% because the bran adds more pentosans. Stoneground wholemeal rye may need even more, due to finer bran particle distribution.

Do not try to reduce hydration to make rye dough easier to handle — it will not help. An under-hydrated rye dough is dense, coarse, and cracks at the crust. The stickiness is a feature of the grain, not a problem to solve.

docs.title: Handling Rules
docs.text:
Rye dough cannot be handled like wheat dough. The approaches that build strength in wheat — kneading, slap-and-fold, intensive mixing — will only make rye dough wetter and more unworkable.

  1. Mix, do not knead. For up to about 50% rye, a short mix (3–4 minutes) to fully incorporate is enough. For higher percentages, mix until combined and stop.
  2. Wet hands always. Water prevents the dough from sticking to your hands without adding flour, which would change the hydration.
  3. Use a tin for high-rye loaves. Any loaf with more than 60–70% rye will not hold its shape free-standing. Use a well-greased loaf tin; do not attempt to shape and score.
  4. No aggressive folding. A gentle fold or two during early bulk is fine for medium-rye loaves; skip it entirely for high-rye. Unlike wheat, rye dough does not benefit from structure-building folds.
  5. Longer bake, lower finish temperature. The dense, moist crumb of a high-rye loaf takes longer to bake through. 96–98°C internal temperature still applies, but expect a longer bake time than a wheat loaf of equivalent size.

docs.title: Sourdough Fermentation with Rye
docs.text:
Rye ferments faster than wheat, partly because its starch is more accessible to the microorganisms in a sourdough culture, and partly because the flour itself carries more active amylase to pre-digest starches into sugars.

  • Bulk fermentation: At typical UK room temperature (18–22°C) with 15% inoculation, a medium-rye loaf (50% rye) bulk ferments in roughly 3–4 hours. For high-rye loaves (80–100%), bulk fermentation is shorter and less dramatic — the dough will not double; look for small bubbles at the surface and a slight rise of 20–30%.
  • Over-fermentation risk: High-rye doughs that over-ferment become overly acidic and produce a harsh, vinegary bread rather than the balanced earthiness you want. Do not aim for maximum sourness — rye is naturally flavourful and doesn't need pushing.
  • Cold retard: Less critical for rye than for wheat, since there is no gluten to firm up in the cold. Cold retarding can help develop flavour, particularly for medium-rye loaves in a tin. For 100% rye loaves, room-temperature proofing and baking the same day is the traditional approach.
  • Starter choice: A rye starter (see sourdough.md) is the natural pairing for rye bread. A wheat starter works but produces a milder result. Using a peaked rye starter at 15–20% inoculation is a reliable starting point for most rye proportions.

docs.title: Nutrition Note
docs.text:
Rye is arguably the most nutritionally complete grain a home baker can use. Some highlights:

  • Dietary fibre: 15–21% of dry weight, compared to 11–13% for wheat. A larger proportion of rye's arabinoxylan fibre is soluble — meaning it forms a gel in the digestive tract that slows glucose absorption and feeds beneficial gut bacteria.
  • Glycaemic response: Rye produces a meaningfully lower blood glucose spike than equivalent wheat bread, a consistent finding across clinical research. The mechanism is the viscous arabinoxylan and beta-glucan fibre moderating digestion.
  • Satiety: Studies have found that whole grain rye consumption leads to greater reduction in energy intake than whole grain wheat, likely because the viscous fibre produces longer-lasting fullness.
  • Minerals and B vitamins: Rye is a good source of phosphorus, manganese, magnesium, zinc, and B vitamins (B1, B3, B6, folate), especially in wholemeal or dark extraction.

Rye is not gluten-free. It contains secalin, a prolamin closely related to the gliadins in wheat. Secalin triggers celiac disease just as wheat gluten does — rye must be avoided by anyone with celiac disease. Long-fermented sourdough may partially reduce immunoreactivity but does not make rye safe for celiac patients.

docs.title: What Spelt Is
docs.text:
Spelt (Triticum spelta) is an ancient hulled wheat, cultivated for thousands of years before largely being displaced by modern bread wheat. It has seen a revival in artisan baking for its nutty, slightly sweet flavour and perceived digestibility advantages.

Wholemeal spelt retains the bran, germ, and endosperm — giving deeper flavour but demanding more careful handling than white spelt or white wheat.

docs.title: Gluten: The Critical Difference
docs.text:
Spelt's gluten is fundamentally different from modern wheat:

Property Spelt Modern Bread Wheat Gluten strength Weak Strong Extensibility Very high Moderate Elasticity (spring-back) Low High Tolerance to over-handling Poor Good Fermentation speed Faster Slower

Why this matters: Spelt dough feels strong early in bulk fermentation but becomes noticeably slacker as fermentation progresses, because the weaker gluten network degrades faster. This is the primary reason spelt loaves can collapse — over-fermented or over-handled dough loses structure entirely.

Spelt gluten is dominated by gliadins (extensibility proteins), while modern wheat is dominated by glutenins (elasticity proteins). This is why spelt stretches easily but doesn't spring back.

docs.title: Hydration Guidance
docs.text:
Wholemeal spelt absorbs water differently from white flour: - Bran particles absorb water slowly — they need time to fully hydrate - But the gluten network is weaker, so it can't sustain the structure that high hydration requires

Target range: 70–75% hydration for wholemeal spelt.

Starting at 72% is safe for a first bake with a new bag of flour. Once you understand how the dough feels, adjust upward or downward.

Do not chase high hydration — it does not reward you with better structure in spelt the way it can with high-gluten wheat. A tighter, well-fermented dough at 72% will out-perform a slack, over-hydrated one.

docs.title: Handling Rules
docs.text:
1. Minimal mixing — once ingredients are incorporated, stop. Spelt needs roughly half the kneading time of bread wheat (4–6 minutes vs 10–12).
  2. Gentle stretch and folds — no aggressive slap-and-fold or French fold. Use coil folds or standard stretch-and-folds with a light touch.
  3. Stop bulk fermentation earlier — aim for 40–50% volume increase, not the 75–100% you'd target with wheat. The dough will continue to ferment in the cold retard.
  4. Handle the shaped loaf as little as possible — each touch degrades the gluten structure further.
  5. Do not autolyse for longer than 45 minutes — for wholemeal spelt, 30–45 minutes is ideal to hydrate the bran without over-relaxing the already-extensible gluten.

docs.title: Wholemeal vs White Spelt
docs.text:
Wholemeal Spelt White Spelt Flavour Deeper, nuttier, more complex Milder, cleaner Hydration needed Slightly higher (bran absorbs) Slightly lower Gluten behaviour Bran cuts gluten strands = weaker structure Stronger than wholemeal Rise Lower, denser crumb More open crumb possible Handling Most delicate More forgiving

For open crumb and high oven spring with spelt, a blend (e.g. 70% wholemeal + 30% white spelt, or adding some strong bread wheat) gives more headroom. A 100% wholemeal loaf will be denser — this is expected and correct, not a failure.

docs.title: Sourdough Fermentation with Spelt
docs.text: Spelt ferments faster than modern wheat because its starch is more accessible to the wild yeast and bacteria in a sourdough culture. In practice: - Reduce your bulk fermentation time by ~20–30% compared to an equivalent wheat recipe - Be more vigilant about dough temperature — a warm kitchen (24°C+) can push a spelt dough past its ideal window quickly - The sweet flavour of spelt pairs well with a mild sourdough sourness; a lower inoculation (10–15%) and cold retard give the best flavour balance

docs.title: Nutrition Note
docs.text: Long-fermented spelt sourdough (12+ hours total) is low in FODMAPs due to the breakdown of fermentable carbohydrates by the sourdough bacteria. This can make it better tolerated by people with IBS or wheat sensitivity — though it is not gluten-free and is not suitable for coeliac disease.

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text: Recipe Description Wholemeal Spelt Sourdough 100% wholemeal spelt sourdough — a good starting point if you're new here White Sourdough High-hydration open-crumb white sourdough (80%, 90/10 flour blend) Pain de Campagne Rustic country loaf on a rye sourdough starter (78%, 85/10/5 flour blend)

docs.title: Pain de Campagne
docs.text:
A rustic French country loaf built on a rye sourdough starter. The flour blend is mostly white with a measure of wholemeal wheat and a small addition of whole rye, giving the crumb a creamy warmth and subtle earthiness. The rye starter brings a malty, mineral depth and a more complex tang than a white-flour starter can produce — this is a noticeably more interesting loaf to eat than a straight white sourdough, without being challenging to make.

This bread is more forgiving than the white sourdough — 78% hydration makes handling easier, and the rye starter's vigour gives reliable, active fermentation. The tradeoff is a shorter bulk window; watch the dough, not the clock.

docs.title: A Note on Rye Starter Hydration
docs.text:
If you have been maintaining your rye starter at 200% hydration, convert it to 100% before using it for bread. A 200% rye starter is counterproductively liquid: rye's pentosans (complex polysaccharides) absorb water so aggressively that at 200% the starter becomes near-pourable, losing all gas-retention ability, shortening the already-fast peak window, and making it impossible to read when the starter is ready. At 100% hydration — equal weights flour and water — a rye starter is a thick paste, which is correct.

Converting from 200% to 100%: Do 2–3 feeds using equal weights of rye flour and water. After two feeds the culture is effectively at 100% hydration regardless of starting state. It will be noticeably thicker than you are used to.

Ongoing maintenance: Feed with equal weights whole rye flour and water. A rye starter at 100% is vigorous and robust — it tolerates a wider temperature range than a wheat starter, recovers quickly after fridge dormancy, and gives a readable peak: a domed top, bubbles throughout, and slight pulling away from the jar sides.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
All quantities in baker's percentages relative to total flour weight.

Ingredient % Weight (650g flour) Strong white bread flour 85% 553g Wholemeal wheat flour 10% 65g Whole rye flour 5% 32g Water 78% 507g Active rye starter (100% hydration) 15% 100g Fine sea salt 2% 13g

Total dough weight: ~1,273g Expected baked weight: ~1,080g (roughly 15% baking loss)

Water split: 487g into autolyse, 20g reserved for dissolving salt.

Why 15% inoculation? Rye starters are significantly more active than white-flour starters. At 20% inoculation, a rye-starter dough can over-ferment before shaping in a typical kitchen. 15% gives a reliable bulk window of 4–5.5 hours.

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text: To change the loaf size, pick your target flour weight and multiply: - Small loaf (450g flour): multiply all weights by 0.69× - Large loaf (900g flour): multiply all weights by 1.38×

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
• Digital scales (0.1g or 1g precision)
  • Large mixing bowl or lidded container for bulk ferment
  • Round or oval banneton, ~1kg capacity
  • Dutch oven (cast iron, at least 4L)
  • Bench scraper
  • Lame or razor blade for scoring
  • Instant-read thermometer
  • Large, smooth work surface for lamination (wet, not floured)

docs.title: Water Temperature
docs.text:
Target dough temperature after mixing: 24°C.

Water temp = (Target dough temp × 3) − room temp − flour temp − starter temp

In a typical 20°C kitchen: 

Water temp = (24 × 3) − 20 − 20 − 20 = 12°C  →  slightly cool tap water

docs.title: Reviving from dormancy
docs.text:
Revive with 2–3 feeds at room temperature before bake day. Feed at 100% hydration throughout: equal weights whole rye flour and water.

Dormancy Revival feeds Up to 1 week 1–2 feeds 1–3 weeks 2–3 feeds More than 3 weeks 3 feeds minimum; discard deeper if it smells sharp or chemical

Each feed: discard to 20g, add 20g rye flour + 20g water. Leave 8–12 hours between feeds at room temperature.

docs.title: Build feed
docs.text:
Once reliably active (doubling within 6–8 hours), do one larger feed to produce the 100g needed:

  • Discard to 20g
  • Add 50g rye flour + 50g water
  • Total produced: ~120g — use 100g in the dough, keep 20g as ongoing culture

Leave 5–8 hours at room temperature until peaked.

docs.title: Signs of readiness
docs.text:
• Doubled (or nearly doubled) from fed weight — rye rises less dramatically than wheat; 1.5–2× is normal
  • Top is domed, not flat or sunken
  • Interior full of bubbles throughout; smells tangy, yeasty, and slightly malty
  • Pulls away from the sides of the jar
  • Float test: a small spoonful dropped in water floats

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text:
This assumes a starter at peak. The schedule targets an overnight cold proof and baking the following morning.

Step Time (approx) Autolyse T + 0:00 Add starter T + 1:00 Add salt T + 1:30 Stretch and fold set 1 T + 2:00 Stretch and fold set 2 T + 2:30 Lamination T + 3:00 Coil fold 1 T + 3:30 Coil fold 2 T + 4:00 End of bulk ferment T + 4:30 – 5:30 Pre-shape + bench rest When bulk is complete Final shape ~30 min after pre-shape Into fridge ~5 min after final shape Bake next morning T + 14:00 – 20:00

The bulk window is shorter than for white sourdough. Target 50% volume rise, not 75%. A rye starter runs fast; pushing further produces a slack, over-acidic dough that will spread rather than rise.

docs.title: 1. Autolyse (T + 0:00)
docs.text:
• Combine 553g strong white flour, 65g wholemeal wheat flour, and 32g whole rye flour in a large bowl; mix the dry flours together first
  • Add 487g water and mix by hand until no dry flour remains — 1–2 minutes
  • Cover and rest 45–60 minutes at room temperature

docs.title: 2. Add Starter (T + 1:00)
docs.text:
• Pour 100g peaked rye starter over the autolysed dough
  • Incorporate using the pincer method: squeeze through the dough with your fingers, alternating with folding, until no streaks remain — ~2–3 minutes
  • Cover and rest 30 minutes

The dough will feel extensible but less wet than an 80% hydration white dough — the rye flour adds absorbency. This is correct.

docs.title: 3. Add Salt (T + 1:30)
docs.text:
• Dissolve 13g fine sea salt in the reserved 20g water
  • Pour over the dough and incorporate with the pincer method until fully mixed — ~1–2 minutes
  • Cover and rest 30 minutes

docs.title: 4. Stretch and Folds — Sets 1 and 2 (T + 2:00 and T + 2:30)
docs.text:
Perform 2 sets of stretch and folds, 30 minutes apart.

One set: 1. Wet your hand 2. Grab the far side of the dough, stretch up until resistance, fold to the near side 3. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat — four folds total = one set 4. Cover and rest 30 minutes

docs.title: 5. Lamination (T + 3:00)
docs.text:
1. Wet the work surface generously — the dough should slide freely
  2. Pour the dough out onto the wet surface
  3. Stretch outward in all directions until a large, thin rectangle — aim for almost translucent in the thinnest areas
  4. If it tears before fully stretched, rest 5 minutes and continue
  5. Fold one third over the centre (letter fold), then fold the opposite third on top
  6. Rotate 90° and letter-fold again
  7. Return to the container; cover and rest 30 minutes

docs.title: 6. Coil Folds (T + 3:30 onwards)
docs.text:
Perform 2 sets of coil folds, 30 minutes apart.

One coil fold: 1. Slide both hands under the centre of the dough and lift gently 2. Let one end fall and fold under, then lower and let the other end fold under 3. Rotate 90° and repeat once

After the second set, leave the dough completely undisturbed for the remainder of bulk fermentation.

docs.title: 7. Bulk Fermentation
docs.text:
The dough is ready to shape when:

  • Volume has increased by 50%
  • The surface looks gently domed and bubbly, with visible gas just beneath
  • The dough moves as a cohesive, airy mass when the container is jiggled
  • A finger poke springs back slowly, but not all the way

At 20–22°C, this typically occurs 4–5.5 hours after the starter was added (T + 4:30 to T + 5:30). Warmer kitchens shorten this significantly — a rye starter in a 24°C kitchen can complete bulk in 3.5 hours.

Do not push to 75% rise. With a rye starter, 50% is the target. Over-fermentation produces a dough too slack and acidic to hold structure in the oven.

docs.title: 8. Pre-Shape
docs.text:
1. Gently scrape the dough onto a lightly floured surface
  2. Using a bench scraper in one hand and your other hand on top, drag the dough toward you to build surface tension
  3. Repeat 2–3 times, rotating slightly each time, until the dough forms a taut round
  4. Cover with an inverted bowl or damp cloth and bench rest 25–30 minutes

docs.title: 9. Final Shape
docs.text:
Both boule (round) and bâtard (oval) work well for pain de campagne.

Boule (classic look): 1. Flour a round banneton generously with rice flour 2. Flip the dough — smooth side down, rough side up 3. Draw the edges up into the centre, then flip and rotate with cupped hands to build surface tension 4. Place seam-side up in the banneton; pinch the seam gently to seal

Bâtard (same method as white sourdough): 1. Flour an oval banneton generously with rice flour 2. Flip the dough; stretch gently into a rough rectangle 3. Fold the left and right sides in to the centre 4. Roll from the top toward you, dragging back slightly to build tension as you roll 5. Place seam-side up in the banneton

Cover with a shower cap or cling film.

docs.title: 10. Cold Retard
docs.text:
• Place the covered banneton in the fridge at 4–5°C
  • Leave 10–16 hours — overnight is ideal; bake straight from the fridge without warming

The cold retard is particularly important with a rye starter. The slow, cool fermentation develops earthy, complex depth that makes this loaf genuinely interesting — it is the biggest single driver of flavour. A longer retard (14–16 hours) gives a more pronounced character; 10 hours will be cleaner and milder.

docs.title: 11. Baking
docs.text:
Preheat: Dutch oven (with lid) in the oven at 250°C (230°C fan / 480°F) for at least 45 minutes.

Score: Invert the banneton onto a piece of baking parchment. Score immediately:

  • Single ear cut: one deep cut at 30–45° along the length of a bâtard — dramatic, gives a strong ear
  • Cross cut: two cuts crossing in the centre of a boule — the classic pain de campagne look
  • Wheat stalk: a long central cut with short diagonal cuts branching off each side — rustic and distinctive

Cut swiftly and decisively.

Into the oven: 1. Lift the dough using the parchment, lower carefully into the hot Dutch oven 2. Replace the lid immediately

Phase Temperature Time Lid on (steam) 250°C / 230°C fan 20 min Lid off (crust) 220°C / 200°C fan 25–30 min

The rye content promotes slightly faster Maillard browning than a straight white loaf — a darker bake is correct here. The loaf is done when: - Sounds hollow when tapped on the base - Internal temperature: 96–98°C - Deep golden-brown to dark crust

Cool on a wire rack for at least 1.5 hours before slicing.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
• Crumb: Semi-open, irregular holes; creamy to slightly warm-toned colour from the rye and wholemeal. Less dramatically open than pure white sourdough, but far from dense
  • Crust: Thick and crackly, often developing a fine craze pattern as it cools
  • Flavour: Earthy, malty depth from the rye starter, with a well-rounded tang and nutty sweetness from the wholemeal — more complex than white sourdough; flavour peaks on day 2 and holds well through day 3
  • Shelf life: 4–5 days well wrapped; the rye content improves moisture retention compared to a straight white loaf

docs.title: Variations
docs.text: Variation Change More rye character Increase rye in dough to 10–15%; reduce white flour accordingly; expect a slightly denser, more flavourful crumb More open crumb Remove rye from dough entirely (90% white, 10% wholemeal); handle as white sourdough More tang Extend cold retard to 16–18 hours; or build a stiff rye levain (60% hydration) 12 hrs before mixing Milder, sweeter Reduce cold retard to 10 hours; catch the starter just before peak Caraway Add 1–2% caraway seeds (7–13g) with the salt — pairs beautifully with the rye flavour Seeded crust Roll the shaped loaf in mixed seeds (sesame, sunflower, nigella) before placing seam-side up in the banneton

docs.title: White Sourdough
docs.text:
A high-hydration white sourdough with an open, irregular crumb and thick, deeply coloured crust — the kind of loaf you'd find at a German farmer's market. The blend of strong white bread flour with 10% wholemeal wheat adds fermentation activity, subtle flavour depth, and a creamy off-white crumb colour without compromising the open structure.

This is a more demanding bake than the wholemeal spelt loaf — the dough is wetter and more extensible, requiring confident handling and good timing. The reward is a loaf with a dramatic ear, large uneven holes, and a mild, complex flavour.

Use the strongest white bread flour you can find (12–13% protein). Supermarket bread flour works, but a strong Canadian or high-protein milling flour will give noticeably better oven spring and a more open crumb.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
All quantities in baker's percentages relative to total flour weight.

Ingredient % Weight (650g flour) Strong white bread flour 90% 585g Wholemeal wheat flour 10% 65g Water (total) 80% 520g Active starter (100% hydration, white flour) 20% 130g Fine sea salt 2% 13g

Total dough weight: ~1,313g Expected baked weight: ~1,115g (roughly 15% baking loss)

Water split: 500g into autolyse, 20g reserved for dissolving salt.

The starter is maintained and fed on white bread flour throughout — the wholemeal character comes from the dough flour only.

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text: To change the loaf size, pick your flour weight and multiply: - Small loaf (450g flour): multiply all weights by 0.69× - Large loaf (900g flour): multiply all weights by 1.38×

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
• Digital scales (0.1g or 1g precision)
  • Large mixing bowl or lidded container for bulk ferment
  • Oval banneton (proofing basket), ~1kg capacity — or a bowl lined with a well-floured cloth
  • Dutch oven (cast iron, at least 4L)
  • Bench scraper
  • Lame or razor blade for scoring
  • Instant-read thermometer
  • Large, smooth work surface for lamination (wet, not floured)

docs.title: Water Temperature
docs.text:
Target dough temperature after mixing: 24°C.

Use the following formula to calculate water temperature: 

Water temp = (Target dough temp × 3) − room temp − flour temp − starter temp
In a typical 20°C kitchen with flour at 20°C and starter at 20°C: 
Water temp = (24 × 3) − 20 − 20 − 20 = 72 − 60 = 12°C  → slightly cool tap water
Adjust for your conditions. In a warm kitchen, use cold water or add ice. Temperature control matters — 2°C can shift your bulk window by 30–60 minutes.

docs.title: Starter Revival & Preparation
docs.text: If your starter has been dormant in the fridge, revive it before baking day. Feed on white bread flour throughout — revival and build feeds alike.

docs.title: Revival feeds
docs.text:
Each feed: discard to 25g, add 25g water + 25g white bread flour (1:1:1 by weight). Leave 10–12 hours at room temperature (20–22°C) between feeds.

Dormancy Number of revival feeds Up to 2 weeks 2 feeds More than 2 weeks 3 feeds

A sluggish starter can be encouraged by adding a pinch of wholegrain rye flour to one feed — the extra minerals spur fermentation activity.

docs.title: Build feed
docs.text:
Once the starter reliably doubles within 4–6 hours of a feed, do one final larger feed to produce the 130g needed for the recipe:

  • Discard to 45g
  • Add 65g water + 65g white bread flour
  • Total produced: ~175g — use 130g in the dough, keep 45g as ongoing starter

Leave 4–8 hours at room temperature until peaked.

docs.title: Signs of readiness
docs.text:
• Doubled from its fed weight
  • Top is domed, not flat or sunken
  • Interior is bubbly and webby when stirred
  • Smells tangy and yeasty — not sharp, alcoholic, or like nail varnish
  • Float test: a small spoonful dropped in water floats

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text:
This recipe assumes you are starting with a starter at peak (just doubled in size). The schedule below targets a same-day bulk fermentation and overnight cold proof for baking the next morning.

Step Time (approx) Autolyse T + 0:00 Add starter T + 1:00 Add salt T + 1:30 Stretch and fold set 1 T + 2:00 Stretch and fold set 2 T + 2:30 Lamination T + 3:00 Coil fold 1 T + 3:30 Coil fold 2 T + 4:00 Coil fold 3 (if needed) T + 4:30 End of bulk ferment T + 5:30 – 7:00 Pre-shape + bench rest When bulk is complete Final shape ~30 min after pre-shape Into fridge (cold retard) ~5 min after final shape Bake next morning T + 15:00 – 20:00

All times are approximations. Watch the dough, not the clock.

docs.title: 1. Autolyse (T + 0:00)
docs.text:
• Weigh 585g strong white bread flour and 65g wholemeal wheat flour into a large bowl and mix the two flours together
  • Add 500g water and mix by hand until no dry flour remains — 1–2 minutes, no more
  • Cover and rest at room temperature for 45–60 minutes

White flour's stronger gluten tolerates a longer autolyse than spelt — the full 60 minutes makes a high-hydration dough significantly more manageable and extensible.

Why hold back water? The reserved 20g is used to dissolve the salt and introduce it evenly without having to over-work the dough.

docs.title: 2. Add Starter (T + 1:00)
docs.text:
• Pour 130g of peaked active starter over the autolysed dough
  • Incorporate using the pincer method: squeeze through the dough with your fingers, alternating with folding — repeat until no streaks remain (~2–3 minutes)
  • Cover and rest 30 minutes

The dough will feel extensible and quite wet. At 80% hydration this is correct — it will tighten considerably once salt is added and as the fold sequence progresses.

docs.title: 3. Add Salt (T + 1:30)
docs.text:
• Dissolve 13g salt in the reserved 20g water
  • Pour over the dough
  • Incorporate with the pincer method until fully mixed — ~1–2 minutes
  • The dough will feel noticeably tighter and more cohesive after salt is added
  • Cover and rest 30 minutes

docs.title: 4. Stretch and Folds — Sets 1 and 2 (T + 2:00 and T + 2:30)
docs.text:
Perform 2 sets of stretch and folds, spaced 30 minutes apart.

One set: 1. Wet your hand to prevent sticking 2. Grab the dough on the far side, stretch up until resistance, fold over to the near side 3. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat — four folds in total = one set 4. Cover and rest 30 minutes

These two sets build the initial gluten network before the more intensive lamination step.

docs.title: 5. Lamination (T + 3:00)
docs.text:
Lamination is the key technique for developing structure and an open crumb in high-hydration white sourdough. Performed once, after the two stretch and fold sets.

  1. Wet your work surface generously — the dough should slide, not stick
  2. Pour the dough out onto the wet surface
  3. Using both hands, gently stretch the dough outward in all directions until it forms a large, thin rectangle — aim for almost translucent in places
  4. If the dough tears before it is fully stretched, stop and rest it for 5 minutes before continuing
  5. Fold one third over the centre (letter fold), then fold the opposite third on top
  6. Rotate 90° and letter-fold again
  7. Return the dough to its container
  8. Cover and rest 30 minutes

After lamination the dough is delicate and should not be stretched aggressively. Continue only with coil folds from this point.

docs.title: 6. Coil Folds (T + 3:30 onwards)
docs.text:
Perform 2–3 sets of coil folds, spaced 30 minutes apart. A third set is only needed if the dough still feels notably slack after the second.

One coil fold: 1. Slide both hands under the centre of the dough, lift gently 2. Let one end fall and fold under, then lower and let the other end fold under 3. Rotate 90° and repeat once — that is one coil fold complete

After the final set, leave the dough completely undisturbed for the remainder of bulk fermentation.

docs.title: 7. Bulk Fermentation (until ready to shape)
docs.text:
The dough is ready to shape when:

  • Volume has increased by 50–75%
  • The surface looks domed and bubbly, with visible gas just beneath
  • The edges of the dough against the container are slightly domed
  • When jiggled, the dough moves as a cohesive, airy mass
  • A finger poke springs back slowly, but not all the way

At 21–22°C, this typically occurs 4.5–6 hours after the starter was added (T + 5:30 to T + 7:00 overall). Warmer kitchens shorten this; cooler kitchens lengthen it.

Do not over-ferment. Over-fermentation at 80% hydration produces a very slack, hard-to-shape dough that will spread rather than rise in the oven. The 50–75% rise target is intentionally conservative — the loaf continues proofing during the cold retard.

docs.title: 8. Pre-Shape
docs.text:
1. Gently scrape the dough onto a lightly floured surface
  2. Using a bench scraper in one hand and your other hand on top, drag the dough towards you in a smooth motion to build surface tension
  3. Repeat 2–3 times, rotating slightly each time, until the dough forms a taut round
  4. Cover with an inverted bowl or damp cloth and bench rest for 25–30 minutes

docs.title: 9. Final Shape — Bâtard
docs.text:
A bâtard (oval) is recommended — it produces the most characteristically German artisan look, a dramatic ear, and a better crumb structure for a large loaf.

  1. Flour your oval banneton generously with rice flour (it doesn't absorb and prevents sticking far better than wheat flour)
  2. Flip the dough — smooth side down, rough side up
  3. Gently stretch into a rough rectangle
  4. Fold the left and right sides in to the centre
  5. Roll from the top towards you, using your hands to drag back slightly and build tension as you roll
  6. Place seam-side up in the banneton
  7. Pinch the seam gently to seal, dust the top with rice flour
  8. Cover with a shower cap, cling film, or damp cloth

Shape firmly and with purpose. High-hydration dough rewards decisiveness — one confident pass is far better than repeated adjustments.

docs.title: 10. Cold Retard
docs.text:
• Place the covered banneton in the fridge at 4–5°C
  • Leave for 10–16 hours — overnight is ideal
  • Do not let it warm up before baking; bake straight from the fridge

White flour's stronger gluten holds well through a longer retard than spelt. The extended cold develops a mild acidity that gives a proper artisan loaf its backbone. A longer retard (14–16 hours) will give a slightly more pronounced sour note; 10 hours will be milder and sweeter.

docs.title: 11. Baking
docs.text:
Preheat: Place the Dutch oven (with lid on) in the oven and preheat to 250°C (230°C fan / 480°F) for at least 45 minutes. The thermal mass must be fully saturated — do not rush this.

Score: Remove the dough from the fridge. Cut a piece of baking parchment to fit the base of your Dutch oven. Invert the banneton onto the parchment — the smooth side is now up. Score immediately: - A single deep ear cut along the length of the bâtard, at 30–45° to the surface — this is the classic cut and produces a dramatic ear - Or two to three parallel diagonal cuts for a more rustic German look - Cut decisively and swiftly — hesitation drags rather than slices

Into the oven: 1. Lift the dough using the parchment, lower carefully into the hot Dutch oven 2. Replace the lid immediately

Phase Temperature Time Lid on (steam phase) 250°C / 230°C fan 20 min Lid off (crust phase) 220°C / 200°C fan 25–30 min

The extra time uncovered (compared to the spelt recipe) builds the thick, deeply coloured crust characteristic of a German artisan loaf.

The loaf is done when: - Sounds hollow when tapped on the base - Internal temperature: 96–98°C - Deep golden to dark mahogany crust — do not pull early; colour means flavour

Cool on a wire rack for at least 1.5 hours before slicing. The open crumb of a high-hydration loaf takes longer to set than a tighter crumb. Cutting early will produce a gummy, collapsing interior.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
• Crumb: Large, irregular holes with thin, almost translucent crumb structure between them — more open near the crust, slightly tighter toward the centre
  • Crust: Thick, crackly, deep golden-brown
  • Colour: Creamy off-white from the wholemeal addition
  • Flavour: Clean wheaty depth, mild tang, slightly sweet — flavour peaks on day 2
  • Shelf life: 3–4 days well wrapped; excellent toast on days 3–4

docs.title: Variations
docs.text: Variation Change More open crumb Increase hydration to 82–84%; use high-protein Canadian flour More sour flavour Extend cold retard to 16–18 hours; or reduce inoculation to 15% for slower fermentation Milder, sweeter flavour Reduce cold retard to 10 hours; 20% inoculation keeps fermentation clean Faster same-day bake Increase starter to 25%, skip cold retard, proof at room temperature ~2 hours after shaping More wholemeal character Increase wholemeal wheat to 20%, reduce white to 80% — expect a slightly tighter crumb Seeded crust Spray the scored dough with water and roll in sesame or caraway seeds before loading

docs.title: Wholemeal Spelt Sourdough
docs.text: A 100% wholemeal spelt sourdough loaf using traditional techniques. Dense and flavourful with a crackling crust — this is what spelt bread is meant to taste like. Do not expect the open crumb of a high-gluten wheat loaf; spelt's nature is a tighter, more even crumb.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
All quantities in baker's percentages relative to flour weight.

Ingredient % Weight (450g flour) Wholemeal spelt flour 100% 450g Water (total) 73% 329g Active sourdough starter (100% hydration) 15% 68g Fine sea salt 2% 9g

Total dough weight: ~856g Expected baked weight: ~720g (roughly 15% baking loss)

docs.title: Scaling
docs.text: To change the loaf size, pick your flour weight and multiply: - Small loaf (350g flour): multiply all weights by 0.78× - Large loaf (600g flour): multiply all weights by 1.33×

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
• Digital scales (0.1g or 1g precision)
  • Large mixing bowl or lidded container for bulk ferment
  • Banneton (proofing basket), ~700g capacity — or a bowl lined with a well-floured cloth
  • Dutch oven (cast iron, at least 4L)
  • Bench scraper
  • Lame or razor blade for scoring
  • Instant-read thermometer

docs.title: Water Temperature
docs.text:
Target dough temperature after mixing: 24°C.

Use the following formula to calculate water temperature: 

Water temp = (Target dough temp × 3) − room temp − flour temp − starter temp
In a typical 20°C kitchen with flour at 20°C and starter at 20°C: 
Water temp = (24 × 3) − 20 − 20 − 20 = 72 − 60 = 12°C  → slightly cool tap water
Adjust for your conditions. This matters more in hot weather.

docs.title: Starter Revival & Preparation
docs.text: If your starter has been dormant in the fridge, revive it before baking day.

docs.title: Revival feeds
docs.text:
Each feed: discard to 25g, add 25g water + 25g flour (1:1:1 by weight). Leave 10–12 hours at room temperature (20–22°C) between feeds.

Dormancy Number of revival feeds Up to 2 weeks 2 feeds More than 2 weeks 3 feeds

A sluggish starter can be encouraged by adding a pinch of wholegrain rye flour to one feed — the extra minerals spur fermentation.

docs.title: Build feed
docs.text:
Once the starter reliably doubles within 4–6 hours of a feed, do one final larger feed to produce the 68g needed for the recipe:

  • Discard to 30g
  • Add 30g water + 30g flour
  • Total produced: ~90g — use 68g in the dough, keep 22g as ongoing starter

Leave 4–8 hours at room temperature until peaked.

docs.title: Signs of readiness
docs.text:
• Doubled from its fed weight
  • Top is domed, not flat or sunken
  • Interior is bubbly and webby when stirred
  • Smells tangy and yeasty — not sharp, alcoholic, or like nail varnish
  • Float test: a small spoonful dropped in water floats

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text:
This recipe assumes you are starting with a starter at peak (just doubled in size). The schedule below targets a same-day bulk fermentation and overnight cold proof for baking the next morning.

Step Time (approx) Autolyse T + 0:00 Add starter T + 0:45 Add salt T + 1:15 Stretch and fold set 1 T + 1:30 Stretch and fold set 2 T + 2:00 Stretch and fold set 3 T + 2:30 End of bulk ferment T + 4:30 – 5:30 Pre-shape + bench rest T + 4:30 Final shape T + 5:00 Into fridge (cold retard) T + 5:10 Bake next morning T + 16:00 – 20:00

All times are approximations. Watch the dough, not the clock.

docs.title: 1. Autolyse (T + 0:00)
docs.text:
• Weigh 450g wholemeal spelt flour into a large bowl
  • Weigh 314g water (hold back 15g for dissolving salt later)
  • Mix by hand until no dry flour remains — take ~1–2 minutes, no more
  • Cover and rest at room temperature for 30–45 minutes

The dough will feel rough and shaggy at first. After autolyse it will be smoother, slightly sticky, and extensible.

Why hold back water? The reserved 15g is used to dissolve the salt and add it in a controlled way. It also allows you to adjust hydration if the dough feels too wet or dry after autolyse.

docs.title: 2. Add Starter (T + 0:45)
docs.text:
• Pour 68g of peaked active starter over the autolysed dough
  • Incorporate using the pincer method: squeeze the starter through the dough with your fingers, alternating with folding — repeat until no streaks remain (~2–3 minutes)
  • Cover and rest 30 minutes

The dough should feel fairly extensible. If it tears easily or feels very fragile, handle even more gently from this point.

docs.title: 3. Add Salt (T + 1:15)
docs.text:
• Dissolve 9g salt in the reserved 15g water
  • Pour over the dough
  • Incorporate with the pincer method until fully mixed — ~1 minute
  • The dough will feel noticeably firmer and more cohesive after salt is added
  • Cover and rest 15 minutes

docs.title: 4. Stretch and Folds (T + 1:30 to T + 2:30)
docs.text:
Perform 3 sets of stretch and folds, spaced 30 minutes apart.

One set: 1. Wet your hand to prevent sticking 2. Grab the dough on the far side, stretch up until resistance, fold over to the near side 3. Rotate the bowl 90° and repeat — four folds in total = one set 4. Cover and rest 30 minutes

For spelt, be gentle. These folds build structure — but aggressive handling will destroy it. If the dough feels fragile or tears, do fewer folds per set, or switch to coil folds.

Coil fold alternative (gentler): 1. Slide both hands under the centre of the dough, lift gently 2. Let one end fall and fold under, then lower and let the other end fold under 3. Rotate 90° and repeat once — one coil fold complete

After the 3rd set, leave the dough completely undisturbed.

docs.title: 5. Bulk Fermentation (until ready to shape)
docs.text:
The dough will continue fermenting after the last fold. It is ready to shape when:

  • Volume has increased by ~40–50% (spelt: not more)
  • The surface looks bubbly and alive
  • The edges of the dough against the container are slightly domed
  • When jiggled, the dough moves as a cohesive, airy mass
  • The dough feels lighter and more pillowy than at the start

At 20°C, this typically occurs 3.5–5 hours after the starter was added. In a warmer kitchen (23–24°C), expect 2.5–3.5 hours.

Spelt warning: Do not chase a 75–100% rise as you would with wheat. Spelt's weaker gluten means the dough will become unshapeable if taken that far. A 40–50% rise is correct and intentional.

docs.title: 6. Pre-Shape
docs.text:
1. Turn the dough onto a clean, unfloured surface (slight stickiness helps build tension)
  2. Using a bench scraper in one hand and your other hand on top, drag the dough towards you in a smooth motion to build surface tension
  3. Repeat 2–3 times, rotating slightly each time, until the dough forms a taut round
  4. Cover with an inverted bowl or damp cloth and bench rest for 20–25 minutes

docs.title: 7. Final Shape
docs.text:
1. Flour your banneton generously with rice flour (it doesn't absorb and prevents sticking far better than wheat flour)
  2. Flip the dough — smooth side down, rough side up
  3. Gently stretch into a rough square/rectangle
  4. Fold the top third down, then the bottom third up (letter fold)
  5. Roll from the top towards you, using your hands to drag back slightly and build tension as you roll
  6. Place seam-side up in the banneton
  7. Pinch the seam gently to seal, dust the top (which will become the base) with rice flour
  8. Cover with a shower cap, cling film, or damp cloth

Shape firmly and quickly — one pass is better than fiddling. Every extra touch costs structural integrity.

docs.title: 8. Cold Retard
docs.text:
• Place the covered banneton in the fridge at 4–5°C
  • Leave for 8–16 hours — overnight is ideal
  • Do not let it warm up before baking; bake straight from the fridge

The cold firms the dough, making it easier to score cleanly and giving better oven spring. Acetic acid production increases at cold temperatures, contributing a pleasant mild tang.

docs.title: 9. Baking
docs.text:
Preheat: Place the Dutch oven (with lid on) in the oven and preheat to 250°C (230°C fan / 480°F) for at least 45 minutes. The thermal mass must be fully saturated.

Score: Remove the dough from the fridge. Cut a piece of baking parchment to fit the base of your Dutch oven. Invert the banneton onto the parchment — the smooth side is now up. Score immediately using a lame or sharp razor: - A single curved ear score at ~30–45° to the surface is classic - Or a simple cross if you prefer a more rustic look - Cut confidently and quickly — hesitation produces a dragging score, not a clean one

Into the oven: 1. Lift the dough using the parchment, lower carefully into the hot Dutch oven 2. Replace the lid immediately 3. Bake at 250°C with lid on: 20 minutes — steam phase 4. Remove lid, reduce to 220°C (200°C fan) 5. Bake without lid: 20–25 minutes — crust development

The loaf is done when: - Deep golden to dark brown crust - Sounds hollow when tapped on the base - Internal temperature: 96–98°C

Cool on a wire rack for a minimum of 1 hour before slicing. The crumb continues to set as it cools. Cutting early gives a gummy, undercooked texture even when the bread is fully baked.

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
• Crumb: Close, even, and moist — this is correct for 100% wholemeal spelt. Not the open, holey crumb of white wheat sourdough.
  • Crust: Thin, crackly, deeply coloured
  • Flavour: Nutty, slightly sweet, mild tang — spelt's natural sweetness balances the sourdough acidity
  • Shelf life: Excellent. Keeps well wrapped for 4–5 days; flavour improves on day 2

docs.title: Variations
docs.text: Variation Change More open crumb Replace 30% wholemeal spelt with white spelt or strong white wheat More sour flavour Extend cold retard to 18–20 hours, or increase starter to 20% Faster same-day bake Increase starter to 20%, skip cold retard, proof at room temp ~2 hours after shaping Seeds Add 10–15% toasted seeds (sunflower, sesame, caraway) at salt incorporation stage Seeded crust Roll the shaped loaf in seeds before placing in banneton
